questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Efficiency.

learningObjective.statement: Recall the efficiency equation.

questions.stem: Which equation can be used to calculate the efficiency of a device?
questions.question: Which equation can be used to calculate the efficiency of a device?
options: efficiency = useful output energy transfer ÷ total input energy transfer
efficiency = total input energy transfer ÷ useful output energy transfer
efficiency = wasted energy transfer ÷ useful output energy transfer
efficiency = force ÷ distance
questions.correctAnswer: efficiency = useful output energy transfer ÷ total input energy transfer
questions.explanation: Efficiency is the useful output energy transfer divided by the total input energy transfer. It can be multiplied by 100 to give a percentage.

distractorRationales.text: efficiency = total input energy transfer ÷ useful output energy transfer
efficiency = wasted energy transfer ÷ useful output energy transfer
efficiency = force ÷ distance

questions.senNote: Checks: Recall the efficiency equation.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Efficiency.

learningObjective.statement: Calculate percentage efficiency from useful and total energy.

questions.stem: A motor transfers 80 J of energy usefully from 200 J supplied. What is its efficiency?
questions.question: A motor transfers 80 J of energy usefully from 200 J supplied. What is its efficiency?
questions.explanation: Efficiency = useful output ÷ total input = 80 ÷ 200 = 0.40, which is 40%.
questions.senNote: Checks: Calculate percentage efficiency from useful and total energy.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Efficiency.

learningObjective.statement: Convert decimal efficiency to percentage efficiency.

questions.stem: A device has an efficiency of 0.65. What is this efficiency as a percentage?
questions.question: A device has an efficiency of 0.65. What is this efficiency as a percentage?
questions.explanation: To convert a decimal efficiency to a percentage, multiply by 100. 0.65 = 65%.
questions.senNote: Checks: Convert decimal efficiency to percentage efficiency.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Efficiency.

learningObjective.statement: Calculate wasted energy from total and useful energy transfers.

questions.stem: A lamp receives 500 J of electrical energy and transfers 75 J as useful light. How much energy is wasted?
questions.question: A lamp receives 500 J of electrical energy and transfers 75 J as useful light. How much energy is wasted?
questions.explanation: Wasted energy = total input energy - useful output energy = 500 - 75 = 425 J.
questions.senNote: Checks: Calculate wasted energy from total and useful energy transfers.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Efficiency.

learningObjective.statement: Calculate useful output energy from percentage efficiency.

questions.stem: A machine is 30% efficient. It receives 600 J of energy. How much energy is transferred usefully?
questions.question: A machine is 30% efficient. It receives 600 J of energy. How much energy is transferred usefully?
questions.senNote: Checks: Calculate useful output energy from percentage efficiency.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Efficiency.

learningObjective.statement: Calculate input energy from useful output and efficiency.

questions.stem: A pump is 50% efficient and transfers 300 J usefully. How much energy was supplied to the pump?
questions.question: A pump is 50% efficient and transfers 300 J usefully. How much energy was supplied to the pump?
questions.explanation: Efficiency = useful output ÷ total input, so total input = 300 ÷ 0.50 = 600 J.
questions.senNote: Checks: Calculate input energy from useful output and efficiency.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Efficiency.

learningObjective.statement: Explain why efficiency cannot exceed 100%.

questions.stem: Why can the efficiency of a normal energy transfer device not be greater than 100%?
questions.question: Why can the efficiency of a normal energy transfer device not be greater than 100%?
options: The useful output energy cannot be greater than the total input energy.
All devices destroy their input energy.
Useful output energy is always zero.
Efficiency is measured in joules, so it has no upper limit.
questions.correctAnswer: The useful output energy cannot be greater than the total input energy.
questions.explanation: Energy is conserved. A device cannot transfer more useful energy out than the total energy supplied to it.

distractorRationales.text: All devices destroy their input energy.
Useful output energy is always zero.
Efficiency is measured in joules, so it has no upper limit.

questions.senNote: Checks: Explain why efficiency cannot exceed 100%.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Efficiency.

learningObjective.statement: Interpret arrow width in a Sankey diagram.

questions.stem: In a Sankey diagram, what does the width of an arrow show?
questions.question: In a Sankey diagram, what does the width of an arrow show?
options: The amount of energy transferred.
The speed of the energy transfer.
The temperature of the device.
The voltage across the device.
questions.correctAnswer: The amount of energy transferred.
questions.explanation: In Sankey diagrams, arrow width represents the amount of energy transferred along that pathway.

distractorRationales.text: The speed of the energy transfer.
The temperature of the device.
The voltage across the device.

questions.senNote: Checks: Interpret arrow width in a Sankey diagram.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Efficiency.

learningObjective.statement: Calculate efficiency from values shown on a Sankey diagram.

questions.stem: A Sankey diagram shows 100 J input, 70 J useful output and 30 J wasted output. What is the efficiency?
questions.question: A Sankey diagram shows 100 J input, 70 J useful output and 30 J wasted output. What is the efficiency?
questions.explanation: Efficiency = useful output ÷ total input = 70 ÷ 100 = 0.70 = 70%.
questions.senNote: Checks: Calculate efficiency from values shown on a Sankey diagram.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Efficiency.

learningObjective.statement: Compare efficiencies of two devices.

questions.stem: Device X transfers 40 J usefully from 100 J supplied. Device Y transfers 90 J usefully from 150 J supplied. Which device is more efficient?
questions.question: Device X transfers 40 J usefully from 100 J supplied. Device Y transfers 90 J usefully from 150 J supplied. Which device is more efficient?
options: Device Y
Device X
Both have the same efficiency.
Neither transfers energy usefully.
questions.correctAnswer: Device Y
questions.explanation: Device X is 40% efficient. Device Y is 90 ÷ 150 = 60% efficient, so Device Y is more efficient.

distractorRationales.text: Device X
Both have the same efficiency.
Neither transfers energy usefully.

questions.senNote: Checks: Compare efficiencies of two devices.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Efficiency.

learningObjective.statement: Explain how reducing wasted transfers can improve efficiency.

questions.stem: Which change would usually increase the efficiency of a machine?
questions.question: Which change would usually increase the efficiency of a machine?
options: Reducing wasted energy transfers to the surroundings.
Increasing unwanted heating in the machine.
Making the useful output smaller while keeping the input the same.
Adding friction to moving parts.
questions.correctAnswer: Reducing wasted energy transfers to the surroundings.
questions.explanation: Efficiency increases when a larger fraction of the input energy is transferred usefully, often by reducing wasted transfers.

distractorRationales.text: Increasing unwanted heating in the machine.
Making the useful output smaller while keeping the input the same.
Adding friction to moving parts.

questions.senNote: Checks: Explain how reducing wasted transfers can improve efficiency.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Efficiency.

learningObjective.statement: Explain how lubrication can improve efficiency.

questions.stem: Why can lubricating the moving parts of a machine improve its efficiency?
questions.question: Why can lubricating the moving parts of a machine improve its efficiency?
options: It reduces friction, so less energy is wasted by heating.
It creates extra energy in the machine.
It makes the input energy zero.
It changes wasted sound into voltage.
questions.correctAnswer: It reduces friction, so less energy is wasted by heating.
questions.explanation: Lubrication reduces friction. Less energy is transferred by unwanted heating, so a greater fraction can be useful.

distractorRationales.text: It creates extra energy in the machine.
It makes the input energy zero.
It changes wasted sound into voltage.

questions.senNote: Checks: Explain how lubrication can improve efficiency.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Efficiency.

learningObjective.statement: Explain how insulation reduces wasted energy transfer.

questions.stem: A hot-water tank is insulated. How does this improve efficiency?
questions.question: A hot-water tank is insulated. How does this improve efficiency?
options: Less energy is wasted by heating the surroundings.
More energy is destroyed inside the tank.
The tank no longer needs an energy input.
The water stops having a thermal store.
questions.correctAnswer: Less energy is wasted by heating the surroundings.
questions.explanation: Insulation reduces unwanted energy transfer to the surroundings, so more of the supplied energy remains useful for heating the water.

distractorRationales.text: More energy is destroyed inside the tank.
The tank no longer needs an energy input.
The water stops having a thermal store.

questions.senNote: Checks: Explain how insulation reduces wasted energy transfer.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Efficiency.

learningObjective.statement: Recognise that efficiency is a ratio without a unit.

questions.stem: Which statement about efficiency is correct?
questions.question: Which statement about efficiency is correct?
options: Efficiency has no unit because it is a ratio.
Efficiency is measured in joules.
Efficiency is measured in watts.
Efficiency is measured in metres per second.
questions.correctAnswer: Efficiency has no unit because it is a ratio.
questions.explanation: Efficiency is a ratio of two energy transfers, so the units cancel. It may be given as a decimal or percentage.

distractorRationales.text: Efficiency is measured in joules.
Efficiency is measured in watts.
Efficiency is measured in metres per second.

questions.senNote: Checks: Recognise that efficiency is a ratio without a unit.

questions.stem: A device is 75% efficient. What percentage of its input energy is wasted?
questions.question: A device is 75% efficient. What percentage of its input energy is wasted?
questions.explanation: If 75% is useful, the remaining 25% is wasted because the total input is 100%.
questions.senNote: Checks: Find wasted percentage from efficiency percentage.

learningObjective.statement: Compare useful and wasted transfers using Sankey arrow widths.

questions.stem: A Sankey diagram for a motor has a useful output arrow wider than the wasted output arrow. What does this show?
questions.question: A Sankey diagram for a motor has a useful output arrow wider than the wasted output arrow. What does this show?

The motor has no input energy.

The wasted transfer is larger than the useful transfer.
The motor has no input energy.

questions.senNote: Checks: Compare useful and wasted transfers using Sankey arrow widths.

questions.question: In a closed system, what happens to the total energy?
options: It stays the same.
It always increases.
It is destroyed when objects slow down.
It becomes only useful energy.

From thermal store to elastic potential store.

From thermal store to elastic potential store.
From nuclear store to magnetic store.

It has become gravitational potential energy in the ground.

distractorRationales.text: It has been destroyed during the impact.
It has changed into mass.
